episodes.title: Cut to the Chase! The real signal
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
US headline CPI printed at 4.2% YoY — a 3-year high driven almost entirely by energy — but core inflation came in softer than feared, limiting broadening of price pressures. New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh is expected to hold rates steady through year-end with an initially hawkish tone, while markets repriced: S&P 500 fell 1.6% and long-dated UST yields rose overnight, a risk-off signal with direct EM carry and flow implications.

**Key points**
- Headline CPI +4.2% YoY; energy contributed ~60% of the monthly increase — Middle East conflict is the dominant driver
- Core CPI softer than expected; goods prices contracted, core services eased, shelter inflation declined; consistent with Fed's 2% medium-term target
- Real average hourly earnings -0.7% YoY — consumer income squeeze limits demand-side inflation pass-through
- Oil threshold: if Brent/WTI stays below $120/bbl, StanChart expects inflation to peak in Q2 and converge toward 2% by mid-2027
- Warsh framing: hawkish posture upfront to preserve credibility, but AI-driven productivity gains expected to eventually create space for cuts
- Equity sell-off attributed partly to psychological signal from Magnificent 7 equity issuance and IPO activity, suggesting AI capex cycle is maturing and becoming capital-intensive; further 4–6 week correction expected

**FX / rates implications**
- Softer core CPI limits the urgency for Fed hikes, capping near-term USD upside — a modest relief for EM FX broadly, particularly high-beta LATAM (MXN, BRL) and rate-sensitive EM Asia (INR, IDR)
- Rising long-end UST yields compress EM carry and pressure duration positions across the curve; watch for BI, RBI and Banxico reaction functions if the long end continues to back up
- Equity risk-off and AI-capex maturity signal could trigger EM portfolio outflows, widening credit spreads and pressuring local rates; KRW and TWD most exposed via tech equity linkage
- Oil below $120/bbl keeps commodity-importing EM (INR, PHP, IDR) current accounts from deteriorating sharply

**Watch list**
- Brent/WTI vs. $120/bbl — breach would reset the inflation and EM FX narrative
- Next Fed communications from Warsh for tone shift (hawkish-to-neutral pivot timeline)
- US long-end UST yields: further rise accelerates EM carry unwind
- S&P 500 over the next 4–6 weeks for risk-off depth — key trigger for EM capital flow reversal

episodes.title: Unexpected moves in Asian FX
episodes.feed: Under the Banyan Tree (HSBC Global Research)
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
HSBC's Joey Chu (Head of Asian FX Research) dissects four divergent EM Asia FX stories — USD resilience, JPY fiscal drag, a paradoxical KRW sell-off despite a blowout equity rally, and CNY outperformance driven by strategic oil buffers and de-dollarization — with volatility set to persist on unresolved Middle East risk, Fed uncertainty, and tariff headlines.

**Key points**
- USD supported structurally by US energy self-sufficiency: higher oil prices act as a negative terms-of-trade shock for net-importing Asian economies but not for the US, creating a persistent differential
- JPY under twin pressure: large net oil-import bill plus worsening fiscal trajectory as the government subsidises consumers against energy price spikes, expanding the deficit; BoJ may need to tighten as imported inflation rises
- KRW paradox: KOSPI up ~100% YTD on AI/semiconductor boom, yet foreign investors (owning ~40% of Korean equities) are repatriating profits at a pace that overwhelms Korea's large chip-export trade surplus, keeping the won weak despite strong fundamentals — an unprecedented dynamic
- CNY outperforms because strategic energy stockpiles allow China to draw down inventories rather than buy expensive spot oil; additionally, Chinese exporters are converting USD revenues into CNY at an elevated rate and domestic investors are actively diversifying out of USD assets into gold and domestic securities
- Chinese exporters not losing competitiveness despite firmer CNY — productivity gains absorbing the FX headwind
- Volatility outlook remains elevated: Middle East unresolved, Fed rate-path debated given resilient US growth, and new US tariff announcements flagged as a live risk

**FX / rates implications**
- KRW stays vulnerable: foreign equity ownership ~40% means any continued KOSPI outperformance risks accelerating rebalancing outflows; USDKRW skew to the upside until flows normalise
- JPY bearish bias structural near-term — fiscal deterioration compounds the energy shock; BoJ tightening expectations building but FX relief likely lags
- CNY firmness is a regional divergence signal; limits competitive devaluation pressure from Beijing and provides a partial anchor for EM Asia FX, but watch de-dollarization flow sustainability
- Broad EM Asia FX on the back foot vs USD as long as oil stays elevated; a Middle East de-escalation that compresses energy prices is the clearest catalyst for a reversal

**Watch list**
- Middle East conflict resolution and Brent/WTI price trajectory
- Fed Speakers and US data — any hawkish repricing strengthens USD further vs EM Asia
- US tariff announcements — hosts flag imminent new headlines
- Korea: foreign equity flow data and USDKRW daily fixings
- BoJ policy meetings as JPY inflation pressures build
- PBoC daily CNY fixing and China strategic oil inventory data

episodes.title: Why Tomatoes Are the Most Expensive They've Been in Four Decades
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
An Odd Lots deep-dive into the US fresh tomato supply shock — prices up ~40% YoY to a record $2.69/lb — driven by a combination of extreme weather, US-Mexico anti-dumping tariffs, and surging logistics costs. Relevant to an EM trader primarily as a granular case study in food-CPI dynamics and US-Mexico bilateral trade friction, with downstream read-through to MXN sentiment and EM inflation via food and energy input channels.

**Key points**
- Two Florida freezes this winter wiped out ~80% of the Florida tomato crop (>$150m in losses), removing ~26% of total US supply at a stroke; inelastic demand drove a spot-case price spike to ~$65/case in May vs. a five-year average closer to $20-25.
- Mexico supplies ~70% of US winter tomatoes (up from 20% pre-NAFTA); the US reimposed a 17%+ anti-dumping tariff on Mexican tomatoes in July 2025, cutting Mexican acreage ~3-4% and adding uncertainty vs. the prior suspension-agreement floor system.
- Cross-country trucking costs surged to $16-17k per team-drive (Salinas CA to East Coast) vs. ~$10k eight months prior — attributed to diesel costs and a tighter truck-driver supply linked to immigration enforcement at the border.
- Fertilizer costs are currently locked in for most large growers but contracts renegotiate at end-of-summer; growers are already flagging higher fertilizer input costs for 2026 crop cycles.
- Retailers exhibit asymmetric price pass-through ("up like a rocket, down like a feather"), structurally holding prices above wholesale normalization as digital/logistics overheads permanently raised their cost base.

**FX / rates implications**
- US-Mexico trade friction (anti-dumping tariff on tomatoes) is a small but real signal of continued bilateral trade tension; watch for escalation into broader agricultural or manufactured-goods categories as a MXN headwind.
- Persistent food-CPI stickiness at the retail level — driven by logistics costs tied to oil and structural retailer margin behavior — keeps US headline CPI elevated at the margin, supporting a higher-for-longer Fed narrative that is broadly USD-positive and EM-negative via tighter financial conditions.
- Rising trucking costs (diesel-sensitive) reinforce the Brent/WTI input-cost channel into non-energy CPI; any oil rally amplifies this pass-through.

**Watch list**
- End-of-summer US produce contracting season: grower-distributor fertilizer cost negotiations will signal whether food-CPI re-accelerates into Q4.
- US-Mexico anti-dumping tariff review: any escalation or expansion beyond tomatoes is a direct MXN risk event.
- Trucking spot rates: leading indicator for US PPI goods transport and secondary food-CPI pressure.

episodes.title: The SpaceX IPO drama explained
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
SpaceX is preparing the largest IPO in history, offering only 4% of shares publicly. The episode focuses on two NASDAQ rule changes made one month before the listing: a compressed inclusion timeline (as little as three weeks vs. the previous annual cycle) and a free-float multiplier that will weight SpaceX in the NASDAQ 100 as if 12% of shares trade publicly rather than the actual 4%. For an EM trader, the story is marginal — it is an equity market mechanics piece — but a demand shock of this scale in a single name can absorb liquidity and briefly shift risk sentiment.

**Key points**
- SpaceX IPO is ~4% of total shares; Musk, employees, and private investors retain the rest; no new capital raised, purely a monetisation event for early holders.
- NASDAQ reduced the new-listing waiting period from several months to three weeks for index inclusion.
- Free-float cap change: NASDAQ will weight SpaceX at 3× its actual float (12% vs. 4%), mechanically forcing index funds to buy as if triple the shares exist. Campbell Harvey (Duke) estimates half of initial demand will be index-driven, inflating price before retail can participate.
- S&P 500 has not changed its rules; SpaceX not eligible there under current methodology.
- OpenAI and Anthropic cited as likely future beneficiaries of the same rule changes.

**Watch list**
- SpaceX IPO pricing and first-day trading: a dislocated debut (price spike then collapse) could generate brief risk-off spillover into EM carry and high-beta FX.
- NASDAQ 100 rebalance timing post-listing: watch for forced index-fund buying pressure window (~3 weeks post-IPO).
- Whether S&P 500 revisits its own inclusion criteria; if it does, the capital-flow implications would be considerably larger.

episodes.title: Global FX & Economics: UK Outlook, GBP and SEK
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
JP Morgan's FX Strategy and UK Economics team dissects the UK political transition risk, Bank of England policy path, and relative value across European G10 currencies — primarily GBP, SEK, and NOK. For EM traders, the key read-through is the global carry environment: BoE staying in tightening mode alongside other DM central banks sustains high global yields, which both supports carry strategies broadly and raises the cost of funding in G10 pairs used to finance EM longs.

**Key points**
- UK politics: Makerfield by-election seen as ~70-80% likely Labour win; Andy Burnham expected to win subsequent leadership contest, with timeline pushed to August-September given likely contested race.
- Fiscal risk is contained near-term — Burnham has pledged to honour fiscal rules and the Labour manifesto, though credibility of those pledges under political pressure is questioned; next budget not until end-2026.
- BoE expected to hold next week but vote seen tightening to ~7-2 split (from 6-3); July hike still JP Morgan's base case. Concern centres on energy-driven inflation reaching 3.5-4% by year-end and elevated forward-looking pricing surveys (DMP, PMI, household expectations).
- GBP carry: sterling ranks highest in G10 on systematic carry metrics; EURGBP has ground down toward key support just above 0.8600. Drawn-out political process and BoE tightening bias are frustrating sterling shorts.
- SEK bearish: Swedish retail investors rotated sharply back into foreign/US equities in May, reversing a Q1 repatriation tailwind for SEK. Equity momentum has turned against Sweden since March. SEK carry remains unattractive vs. higher global yields despite marginally firmer Swedish CPI.
- NOK preferred over SEK: Norway CPI print this week reinforces NOK carry advantage.

**FX / rates implications**
- GBP carry longs remain favoured; EURGBP downside toward 0.8600 support is the tactical trade. Any Burnham rhetoric shift toward fiscal expansion post-Makerfield is the key risk that could reprice GBP lower via fiscal risk premium.
- Sustained BoE tightening, in line with global CB direction, keeps global short-end yields elevated — a headwind for EM carry funded via G10, and a reminder that the high-yield EM carry trade is operating in a more competitive yield environment.
- SEK short vs. NOK or GBP remains the preferred expression in European FX space.

**Watch list**
- Makerfield by-election result and Burnham post-victory rhetoric on fiscal policy and snap election timing.
- BoE MPC vote split at next week's meeting — any move toward 6-3 or tighter signals July hike conviction.
- UK CPI prints and energy price trajectory (base case: 3.5-4% by year-end).
- EURGBP 0.8600 support level as a key technical trigger.

episodes.title: MacroVoices #536 Larry Mcdonald: The Migration is Upon us
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Larry McDonald argues markets are entering an inflation-shock 2.0 regime analogous to Q4 2021, driven by a 100-day Strait of Hormuz closure, massive tech IPO supply ($200–250bn immediate raise, ~$3tn in restricted-share unlock by end-2027), and super core CPI annualising at 5.2% — catalysing a "great migration" from financial/growth assets into hard assets, value, energy, materials and healthcare. For EM FX/rates traders the key transmission channels are a dollar re-acceleration, repricing of Fed cut expectations toward a potential hike, and an oil-price coil that could unwind violently.

**Key points**
- S&P 500 at 7,267, testing 50-DMA at 7,300; CTA forced-selling trigger estimated at that level with $100–150bn of systematic selling at risk below it.
- DXY at 100.08, attempting bull breakout; near-term target 101.5, with 102–103 possible if Hormuz remains unresolved.
- UST 10y at 4.53%; market pricing shift from 3 cuts to 1 potential hike; McDonald calls the hike pricing a "facade" given $1.1tn annual federal interest burden, and favours 2s30s steepening via IVOL ETF.
- Super core CPI annualising at 5.2% on last 3 months; Bloomberg Commodities YoY chart described as "blood-curdling" with headline CPI potentially reaching 6–8% in 12 months.
- WTI at $90.03; physical market buffers depleting while Trump jawboning keeps specs underweight — McDonald sees potential for an extremely violent spike once physical rebalancing forces price discovery.
- Gold at $4,133 and still falling; broke below $4,100 support (low $4,047); downside targets $3,900 (Oct lows), $3,400; $3,000 if Hormuz extends to year-end. EM central banks explicitly cited as sellers to raise cash amid the energy shock.
- CCC high-yield spreads blowing out, a leading consumer stress indicator.
- Agnico Eagle (AEM) highlighted as value opportunity at 5.9x EV/EBITDA, down 40%; $6–7bn FCF, $2bn buyback — but McDonald still in "thirds and quarters" scaling mode.

**FX / rates implications**
- DXY break above 100–101 would tighten EM financial conditions broadly: bearish INR, IDR, PHP, MXN, BRL, CLP where carry buffers have compressed; USD strength is the dominant near-term EM macro headwind.
- Fed repricing from cuts to a potential hike compresses rate-differential support for EM carry; short-end UST yield up ~40bp forces reassessment of funded EM longs.
- Oil spike risk is asymmetric for EM oil importers (India, Philippines, Indonesia, Thailand) via wider current-account deficits and pass-through inflation; LATAM commodity exporters (Colombia, Brazil, Peru) partially insulated or net beneficiaries.
- EM central-bank gold selling (explicitly flagged) indicates reserve managers are under liquidity stress — watch for FX intervention drawdowns in vulnerable EM names.
- Gold breakdown reduces a traditional safe-haven bid; if gold tests $3,000, commodity-linked EM currencies (ZAR, PEN) face additional headwind; conversely creates eventual re-entry opportunity.
- 2s30s flattening fade / steepener thesis: if McDonald is right and the Fed cannot hike materially, a subsequent curve re-steepening would ease EM duration pressure and support local-rates longs.

**Watch list**
- FOMC (Worsh's first meeting as new chair) — next week; any hike signal would be a sharp negative for EM risk across the board.
- S&P 500 at 7,300: CTA trigger level; breach would likely see correlated EM risk-off selling.
- DXY 101.5 / 102–103 resistance: a sustained break would accelerate EM FX weakness, especially high-beta names.
- WTI $90 coil resolution: a break above $100 seen as the psychological trigger for a speculative pile-on; watch Hormuz news flow for the catalyst.
- Gold $4,100 re-test from below and $3,900 Oct-low support as short-term bear targets.
- SpaceX IPO timeline (H2 2026) and lock-up schedule (aggressive, into Dec 2026) as a structural equity supply overhang.

episodes.title: Trade among Geopolitical Rivals: Michele Ruta
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
IMF researchers argue that geopolitical rivalry — especially US-China — is undermining the post-WWII trade order built on non-discrimination and reciprocity. Unlike the US-Soviet Cold War (minimal trade links), today's rivals are deeply integrated, making managed disengagement and third-country spillovers central risks for global growth and EM. The paper proposes a "geopolitical exemption" mechanism allowing bilateral disengagement while minimizing damage to third parties.

**Key points**
- US-China bilateral trade flows remain very large despite escalating strategic rivalry; full decoupling is economically costly for both sides
- Two scenarios identified: a "trade war and redemption" path (escalating restrictions, welfare losses for both, eventual painful re-engagement — GATT took ~50 years to liberalize postwar tariffs) vs. a negotiated deal with a formal geopolitical exemption built into WTO architecture
- 2025 tariff escalation explicitly flagged as trajectory toward the trade war path
- Semiconductor and critical-mineral export controls framed as tools designed to keep rivals technologically behind, not merely to protect domestic industry
- Third-country spillovers — particularly relevant for EM supply-chain economies in Asia — highlighted as a major systemic risk in the disengagement scenario

**FX / rates implications**
- Protracted US-China trade war is structurally bearish EM Asia FX: weaker CNY, compressed risk appetite, and supply-chain disruption weigh on KRW, TWD, MYR, THB and VND via electronics and semiconductor exposure
- A negotiated bilateral framework with managed disengagement would be risk-positive and USD-negative through improved global sentiment and CNY stabilization
- Commodity-linked EM (BRL, CLP, COP) face two-way risk: trade war depresses Chinese raw-material demand; any de-escalation reverses that
- Semiconductor export controls remain a structural headwind for TWD and KRW regardless of broader diplomatic tone

**Watch list**
- Any US-China bilateral deal signals or further escalation beyond 2025 tariff baseline
- Critical-mineral and semiconductor export-control announcements from Washington or Beijing
- CNY fixing as a real-time barometer of Beijing's tolerance for trade friction
